Rapid City, South Dakota

A sacred home for peace, harmony & community

Black Hills Hindu Temple & Community Center is a place of worship and gathering for Rapid City's Hindu community, and a welcoming space where neighbors of every background come together in devotion, culture, and fellowship.

Plan Your Visit Get Involved
Shanti

Peace

A quiet space set apart from the everyday, where the mind can settle and the spirit can rest.

Samvaad

Harmony

Many traditions, generations, and backgrounds meeting in one shared home, in balance with one another.

Samaaj

Community

A family that gathers to worship, celebrate, volunteer, and support one another through every season.

Our Story

About the Temple

Founded in 2022, Black Hills Hindu Temple & Community Center was created to give Hindu families in Rapid City and the surrounding Black Hills region a place to worship, mark festivals, and pass traditions on to the next generation.

The temple is run entirely by volunteers from the community and is open to anyone who wishes to visit, observe, or take part — regardless of faith or background. We see the temple as both a spiritual home and a cultural bridge for the region.

Sacred Calendar Festivals & Celebrations
Sacred Calendar

Festivals & Celebrations

Tap any festival to learn what it celebrates. Hindu festival dates follow the lunar-solar calendar, so exact dates are confirmed closer to each year by the Panchang.

Rest of 2026
Jul 29, 2026 Guru Purnima

A day of gratitude for teachers and gurus, honoring the guidance that leads a student from darkness toward light.

Aug 27, 2026 Raksha Bandhan

Sisters tie a protective thread on their brothers' wrists as a symbol of lasting care and support.

Sep 3–4, 2026 Krishna Janmashtami

Celebrates the birth of Lord Krishna with midnight prayers, devotional singing, and a day of fasting.

Sep 14, 2026 Ganesh Chaturthi

A ten-day festival welcoming Lord Ganesha, remover of obstacles, into homes and community spaces.

Oct 11, 2026 Navratri Begins

Nine nights of worship, dance, and fasting honoring the many forms of Goddess Durga.

Oct 20, 2026 Dussehra (Vijayadashami)

Marks the triumph of good over evil and closes out the Navratri season.

Nov 8, 2026 Diwali

The festival of lights, celebrating the return of Lord Rama and the victory of light over darkness.

Nov 9, 2026 Govardhan Puja

Honors Lord Krishna lifting Govardhan Hill to shelter his devotees, observed the day after Diwali.

Nov 10, 2026 Bhai Dooj

Closes the Diwali season with a celebration of the bond between brothers and sisters.

2027
Jan 14, 2027 Makar Sankranti

A solar festival marking the sun's journey northward, celebrated with kites, sweets, and river baths.

~Mar 5, 2027 Maha Shivaratri

A night-long vigil and fast dedicated to Lord Shiva.

~Mar 22, 2027 Holi

The festival of colors, celebrating the arrival of spring and the triumph of good over evil.

~Apr 8, 2027 Ugadi & Chaitra Navratri Begins

The lunar new year for many regions, and the start of Chaitra Navratri's nine nights of worship.

~Apr 14, 2027 Ram Navami

Celebrates the birth of Lord Rama, an embodiment of righteousness and duty.

~May 8, 2027 Akshaya Tritiya

Considered one of the most auspicious days of the year for new beginnings.

~Jul 18, 2027 Guru Purnima

A day of gratitude for teachers and gurus, honoring the guidance that leads a student from darkness toward light.

~Aug 24, 2027 Krishna Janmashtami

Celebrates the birth of Lord Krishna with midnight prayers, devotional singing, and a day of fasting.

~Sep 4, 2027 Ganesh Chaturthi

A ten-day festival welcoming Lord Ganesha, remover of obstacles, into homes and community spaces.

~Sep 30, 2027 Navratri Begins

Nine nights of worship, dance, and fasting honoring the many forms of Goddess Durga.

~Oct 9, 2027 Vijayadashami (Dussehra)

Marks the triumph of good over evil and closes out the Navratri season.

~Oct 28, 2027 Diwali

The festival of lights, celebrating the return of Lord Rama and the victory of light over darkness.

Dates marked with ~ are approximate for 2027, since the official Panchang for that year is typically published in late 2026. We'll update these once confirmed — and specific event details (timing, location at the temple) will be added closer to each date.
